source.title: Forward and backward inference in spatial cognition

confidence_rationale: Rigorous theoretical paper demonstrating that a single probabilistic generative model supports both forward inference (planning, decision making, likelihood of goal under each option) and backward inference (route refinement, motor control signal estimation), with direct mapping to hippocampal theta sequences and remote replay. Strong conceptual support; mapping fidelity is high but this is not empirical evidence — it is theoretical grounding that the dual-mode architecture is computationally feasible in a single substrate.
failure_signatures: If forward and backward inference over the same model require incompatible training objectives (causing catastrophic interference in practice), the single-substrate claim is in trouble even if theoretically elegant.
If replay content is generated by separate circuits rather than the same forward model running in different modes, the shared-weights assumption is violated.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Spatial cognition can be implemented with forward and backward probabilistic inference in a single generative model; theta sequences reflect decision-making (forward inference across options), theta flickering reflects model selection, and remote replay reflects route and motor planning (backward inference to compute control signals).
mapping.ree_translation: E2_x is a single forward-model substrate supporting both Mode 1 (retrospective comparator: backward inference over past action, computing residual) and Mode 2 (prospective evaluator: forward inference over candidate actions, predicting trajectory values). Penny et al. provide the computational proof-of-concept that one model can do both without architectural duplication.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The Penny et al. model is a linear-Gaussian state-space model with closed-form inference; E2_x is a learned neural network with gradient-based training. Whether gradient interference between forward-prediction and backward-credit-assignment training objectives is negligible in a deep network is an open empirical question MECH-257 explicitly flags as its falsifiability criterion.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical computational model with hippocampal data interpretation; no wet-lab data collected in this paper.

confidence_components.notes: PLoS Comp Biol 2013, Burgess lab. High conceptual mapping but is theory, not empirical. Transfer risk: linear-Gaussian vs deep network is a meaningful architectural gap.
